symptom: Newly installed smart thermostat (Nest, Ecobee, Honeywell T-series, etc.) won't power on, reboots repeatedly, loses Wi-Fi, or says 'low battery' despite being hardwired. Older mechanical or battery-only digital stat worked fine on the same wiring. In some cases the HVAC system short-cycles because the thermostat is stealing current through the Rh-to-W loop to charge its internal battery.
diagnosis: Missing or unterminated common (C) wire. Smart thermostats need continuous 24VAC to power the display and Wi-Fi radio; legacy stats ran on two AA batteries or pulled a trickle through the heating/cooling call circuit. Without a dedicated C connection, the new stat either can't stay online or parasitically draws current, which trips the furnace control board's safety timers and causes random cycling.
procedure: Kill power to the furnace / air handler at the service switch AND the breaker. The thermostat runs on 24V but the transformer's primary side is 120/240V.
Pull the thermostat off the wall plate and photograph the existing wiring terminal-by-terminal before disconnecting anything. Label each conductor with tape.

Count the conductors in the cable. A 4-wire cable (R, W, Y, G) is the classic 'no C' install; a 5+ wire cable usually has an unused conductor already pulled — look for a blue or black wire pushed back into the wall.

Option A (spare wire present): at the furnace control board, land the unused conductor on the 'C' or 'Com' terminal of the 24V transformer secondary. At the thermostat end, land the same conductor on C. Done.

Option B (no spare wire, G wire not needed): if the smart stat controls the fan digitally (most do) and the system is single-stage, you can repurpose the green (G/fan) wire as C — but only if you install a manufacturer-supplied fan-relay kit OR the stat supports 4-wire operation natively. Read the install guide; do not guess.

Option C (no spare, all wires needed): install a 24V Fast-Stat or Venstar Add-A-Wire adapter at both ends, OR run a new 18/5 thermostat cable. A power-extender kit (Ecobee PEK, Nest Power Connector) is the faster option for retrofits.
Restore power at the breaker and service switch. Verify 24VAC between R and C at the thermostat baseplate with a multimeter before mounting the stat.
Mount the thermostat, complete its setup wizard, and run a full heating + cooling cycle. Confirm no short-cycling within 30 minutes.
Document the wire mapping on the back of the thermostat plate or in the service record so the next tech isn't guessing.
safety_notes: Always kill 120/240V at the furnace breaker, not just the service switch. Some older installs have the transformer wired hot-side-continuous even when the switch is off.

Never jumper R to C to 'give it power' — this is a dead short across the 24V transformer secondary and will blow the low-voltage fuse on the control board (typical 3A or 5A ATC blade fuse) within seconds.

If the existing transformer is a 20VA unit, adding a smart stat plus a Wi-Fi radio can push total load above its rating, causing nuisance fuse blows. Upgrading to a 40VA replacement transformer (inexpensive part, widely stocked) prevents callbacks.
Verify polarity if the install uses a separate cooling transformer (older dual-transformer systems): Rh and Rc must NOT be jumpered if two transformers are present, or you will short them together.

On heat pump systems, the O/B reversing valve terminal is brand-specific (O = Carrier/Trane cooling-energized; B = Rheem/Ruud heating-energized). Wrong selection in the thermostat setup menu causes the system to heat when the customer asks for cool — a comfort complaint that is easy to miss on a quick commissioning.
